title: Cannot access forwarded ports remotely
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST?**:

/kind bug

**Description**

Ports forwarded to a container with Podman are accessible locally, but cannot be accessed by remote hosts.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. One machine 1: `podman run -d -p 80:80 -v <SOME_LOCAL_DIR>:/usr/share/nginx/html:ro nginx`

2. On machine 1: `iptables -t nat -S CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT` to verify firewall rules are created

3. On machine 1: `telnet localhost 80` to verify local connectivity to HTTP server

4. On another machine: `telnet <IP_OF_MACHINE_1> 80` to verify remote connectivity to HTTP server

**Describe the results you received:**

IPTables output: 
`
-N CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT -p tcp -m comment --comment "dnat name: \"podman\" id: \"3bcb5133e8731ff31cc524756ca7b920bd7d69f0e5b5c675ee99ec0e316bc190\"" -m multiport --dports 80 -j CNI-DN-5d19ae988c93f23cd5102
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT -p udp -m comment --comment "dnat name: \"podman\" id: \"3bcb5133e8731ff31cc524756ca7b920bd7d69f0e5b5c675ee99ec0e316bc190\"" -m multiport --dports 80 -j CNI-DN-5d19ae988c93f23cd5102
`
Local connection to port 80 succeeds, remote connection fails

**Describe the results you expected:**

Local and remote connections both succeed

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

F28 packaged 0.7.1

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: @dcbw PTAL
I made it work with a `registry:2` container running on port 5000, but only with `--network=host`. (openSUSE Leap 15, podman 0.7.2)
Talked with CNI maintainers - it seems like a problem with `firewalld` on Fedora. Not sure what openSUSE ships firewall-wise but it seems like a safe bet it's a conflict with firewall rules.
Huh, good to know. I will check again if the remote machine isn't on Leap 42.3 actually (no firewalld). Thanks, @mheon!

Any progress on this? I just tried out podman and wasted hours on searching for what I did wrong (I just started with containers) before seeing this here. 😒 `docker run -d -p 80:80 nginx` runs fine… Is using `-networking host` a usable workaround or how much unsafe is this really?

I’m on Fedora 28 Atomic Host, Podman v0.8.3 and containernetworking-plugins-0.7.3-1.

Is there a corresponding bug report at CNI?
#1431 might help, though I haven't had a chance to test it for this use case yet

Did a lot of testing with @baude today and #1431 seems to have resolved the issue. It's now merged and released in 0.9.1.1.

@fbruetting I'm going to close this as fixed in 0.9.1.1 - if you can reproduce after upgrading, feel free to reopen
Thank you very much, I’ll test! Do you maybe have an idea of how long it could take to be deployed in F28AH? What’s best to do in the meantime?

@fbruetting Hopefully we'll have a new build landed by the end of this week. If you don't want to wait, once the packages are built today/tomorrow, you should be able to grab them and add them as an rpm-ostree overlay.
`podman run -d -p 80:80 nginx` still doesn’t work on podman v0.9.1-3 from outside localhost, please reopen… Or does this always require `--network host`?

@fbruetting Can you open a new issue, and provide details on your environment there?

This shouldn't require `--net=host` so I'm suspecting it's something to do with iptables and/or firewalld
Tomorrow (and firewalld isn’t active by default on my F28AH system).

I'd be especially interested in `iptables -t nat -nvL` and `iptables -nvl` then. Also - try it immediately after a container is created, before trying any iptables commands (or running anything at all).

Before starting a container (nothing else is active on the system):
```

 pkts bytes target     prot opt in     out     source               destination
```

I can `curl <IP>` locally, but not remote. With Docker it works instead.

Hm. I would expect to see rules added by us in `iptables -nvL` after the container is created, but there's nothing there but the Docker chains. It looks like out iptables rules aren't correctly being added. Can you add `--log-level=debug` to your `podman run` and get the output of that?

Of course, here it is (yes, I forgot to open a new bug… 🤪🙈):

@fbruetting Are you sure this is 0.9.1? It's still running manual IPtables commands, not using our new firewall code - `Running iptables command` no longer appears anywhere in our code...

If this is labelled 0.9.1, we might have a bad build running around somewhere.

Well…:
```
$ podman --version
podman version 0.9.1

$ rpm -q podman
podman-0.9.1-3.gitaba58d1.fc28.x86_64
```

I’m on most-recent Atomic Host:
```
$ rpm-ostree status

State: idle
AutomaticUpdates: disabled
Deployments:
● ostree://fedora-atomic:fedora/28/x86_64/atomic-host
                   Version: 28.20180917.0 (2018-09-17 02:21:22)
                BaseCommit: 17b11d74047ded1264a57555a64ae6fc5d02a332c556679bfcf32864fc91f11e
              GPGSignature: Valid signature by 128CF232A9371991C8A65695E08E7E629DB62FB1
           LayeredPackages: buildah exa fd-find git htop julia neovim powerline-go ripgrep zsh
```

I don’t think that layered Buildah changed anything, as I assume that you run the same.

Wait a second... I don't think the fix landed until 0.9.1.1

That would explain things.
I'll poke people tomorrow and see about getting things bumped to the newer version

So v0.9.1.1 > 0.9.1-3? O_o

But thank you!
`-3` is the RPM version of 0.9.1 - so it'll probably be `0.9.1.1-1` or similar
Ok, easy to misunderstand…
